content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There was a time when being funny required having a thought.
It didn’t have to be groundbreaking. Just a thought. A strange observation about everyday life. A clever comparison. A perfectly timed exaggeration. A joke that made someone pause and think, “That’s actually true.”
Now, apparently, the highest achievement of comedy is repeating a random number, making a distorted AI-generated image, or commenting “bro” under a video until the joke has been drained of its last molecule of meaning.
Welcome to the age of algorithmic humour.
The internet has always been nonsense. Every generation has had its version of absurd comedy. Surreal humour, memes, and ironic stupidity have existed for decades. The problem is not that people are making dumb jokes. Dumb jokes can be brilliant. The problem is that the modern internet has created an environment where the dumbest, emptiest version of humour is often the most rewarded.
One of the strangest developments of modern humour is the replacement of meaning with recognition. A joke no longer has to make sense. It only needs to trigger a feeling of “I have seen this before.”
Take trends like the infamous “67” joke. For anyone who’s outside the loop, it is almost impossible to understand why it is funny. There is no setup or punchline. The humour comes from the collective agreement that it’s funny because everyone else has decided it’s funny.
So essentially, the joke isn’t the number itself. The joke is knowing that there is supposedly a joke.
This phenomenon is not new. People have always had absurd references. Someone saying a random phrase from a popular show or referencing a niche internet meme has always been part of youth culture. Take Michael Scott quotes from The Office, or anything any politician has ever said in the history of ever. But the speed and scale of these jokes has changed.
A joke can now become globally recognizable within days, and then completely meaningless within weeks. Internet humour has turned into fast fashion. Everyone uses the same joke until it becomes embarrassing, then everybody pretends they were never involved.
Then came AI-generated content, which is the perfect machine for creating infinite nonsense. AI has given the internet a factory capable of producing an endless supply of bizarre images, fake stories, strange characters, and meaningless videos.
The result is what people now call “AI brainrot.” It is humor stripped of the human element. The irony is that comedy has always been about humanity. The best jokes reveal something about people. They expose our habits, insecurities, contradictions, and ridiculousness.
AI slop typically does the opposite. It removes people entirely and replaces observation with randomness. It’s not saying, “Look how strange humans are.” It’s saying, “Look, something strange happened. Continue watching.”
For me, the most depressing place to witness the decline of online humour is comment sections.
Once upon a time, comment sections were creative. People would add jokes, create conversations, build on each other’s ideas, or make funny observations. Now, many comments sections have become a competition to repeat the same five reactions.
“Mind you this is my first impression of you.”
“Bro-.”
“And I oop-.”
“I’m early.”
“Louder for the people in the back.”
We’ve created a weird form of comedy where the reaction to the joke becomes more important than the joke itself. People are no longer trying to be funny. They are trying to perform being someone who understands the joke. It’s a form of social imitation. A crowd of people pretending to laugh because they recognise the signal that laughter is expected. And maybe that’s the most human part of all of this.
People want to belong.
The teenager commenting a skull and wilting rose emoji under an absurd video is not necessarily destroying culture. They’re participating in the same social rituals that humans have always participated in. Humans create slang, references, and shared experiences because connection matters.
The problems begin when the shared experience becomes empty.
Humor has always been one of the clearest expressions of personality. You could learn a lot about someone by what they found funny. Someone who loved dry humour saw the world differently from someone who loved absurd comedy. Someone who enjoyed satire shows approached society differently from someone who enjoyed slapstick comedy.
Now, many people are consuming the same jokes from the same platforms at the same time. The algorithm has become the world’s largest comedy club, except the comedian is not a person. It is a system designed to maximize engagement. The result is a sort of cultural flattening where everyone has the same references, everyone repeats the same phrases, and everyone laughs at the same trends. Our humour as a society becomes slightly more interchangeable.
It is easy to criticize younger generations for enjoying different humour. Parents complained about television. Adults complained about video games. Previous generations complained about internet memes. Every generation thinks the next generation’s humour is ridiculous. And sometimes, they’re right.
But the issue is not that young people are laughing at nonsense. Nonsense has always been funny. The issue is that platforms have learned how to mass-produce and distribute the least meaningful forms of nonsense. People are not choosing between brilliant comedy and meaningless comedy in a fair marketplace. They are being placed inside systems that reward whatever keeps attention trapped.
The average person did not build the attention economy, but they are living inside of it.
Despite all of this, humour is not disappearing.
People are still making clever jokes. People are still writing brilliant satire. People are still telling stories, creating characters, and finding new ways to make each other laugh.
Human creativity is far too stubborn to disappear. But good humour requires effort. It requires a perspective, no matter how unique or common.
The funniest people are usually the ones who pay attention to the world around them. They notice the awkward silence in a conversation. They notice the habits people have. They notice contradictions that others tend to ignore.
A computer can generate a billion images in seconds. It still cannot replace someone making a silly joke at the perfect time. This contains the foundation of comedy.
Observation.
The danger of AI slop, Tiktok brainrot, and algorithmic humour is people are forgetting that humour was never supposed to be only noise. The moment humour becomes something we repeat instead of something that we create, we lose something more than just jokes.
We lose a little bit of ourselves.
